profile.name: Dr. Matt Szydagis
roles: Assistant Professor of Physics, University at Albany (SUNY)
First Author, UAPx Field Expedition Paper
organizations: IFEX (Interdisciplinary Research Centre for Extraterrestrial Studies)
profile.summary: Experimental particle physicist known for contributions to the LUX and LZ dark matter direct-detection experiments and creator of NEST (Noble Element Simulation Technique), widely used in particle astrophysics. Pivoted his instrumentation expertise toward UAP research as first author of the first peer-reviewed academic instrumented UAP field expedition paper, co-leading UAPx alongside Kevin Knuth.
early_career: Postdoctoral scholar, UC Davis (2010-2014) - contributed to LUX (Large Underground Xenon) dark matter direct-detection experiment
Created NEST (Noble Element Simulation Technique), open-source software for simulating noble element detector responses, now widely used across the particle physics community

key_events.event: Completed PhD at University of Chicago. Joined UC Davis as postdoctoral researcher on the LUX dark matter experiment - one of the world's most sensitive direct-detection experiments using liquid xenon.

Joined University at Albany (SUNY) physics faculty. Continued contributions to LUX and began development of NEST (Noble Element Simulation Technique), open-source detector simulation software now adopted across the particle astrophysics community.

NEST software widely adopted by the LZ (LUX-ZEPLIN) experiment collaboration and multiple other liquid noble detector projects. Established Szydagis as a leading computational experimentalist in dark matter physics.

Led the first academic instrumented UAP field expedition through UAPx at Laguna Beach, California. The campaign deployed multi-sensor arrays including electromagnetic, optical, and environmental detectors during observation periods.

Affiliated with IFEX (Interdisciplinary Research Centre for Extraterrestrial Studies) at University of Wurzburg, connecting the UAPx program with Hakan Kayal's European satellite-based anomaly detection infrastructure.

Developed C-TAP (Custom Target Analysis Protocol), combining AI with human verification for pixel-level analysis of UAP camera footage. The tool applies detector physics principles to video anomaly detection.

Published as first author on 'Initial Results from the First Field Expedition of UAPx' in Progress in Aerospace Sciences (156:101099), the first peer-reviewed report of an academic instrumented UAP field campaign. Co-authored the flagship 'New Science of UAP' paper in the same journal alongside Kevin Knuth, Hakan Kayal, and Beatriz Villarroel.

associated_people.name: Dr. Kevin Knuth
associated_people.role: Professor of Physics, UAPx Lead Scientist
associated_people.relationship: Institutional colleagues at University at Albany; co-leads UAPx field research program. Szydagis directs the field instrumentation and operational side of UAPx while Knuth leads theoretical physics analysis. Co-authors on both the 2025 field expedition paper and the New Science of UAP framework paper.

associated_people.name: Dr. Beatriz Villarroel
associated_people.relationship: Co-authors on the 2025 'New Science of UAP' paper in Progress in Aerospace Sciences. Villarroel's archival plate transient research and Szydagis's field instrumentation represent complementary empirical approaches within the same collaborative network.

associated_people.name: Prof. Avi Loeb
associated_people.relationship: Both pursue rigorous instrumented approaches to anomalous phenomena detection. UAPx and the Galileo Project represent parallel academic field-research programs; Szydagis is in the same collaborative orbit through IFEX and the 2025 flagship paper.

disclosures.title: Initial Results from the First Field Expedition of UAPx
disclosures.notes: First author. First peer-reviewed report of an academic instrumented UAP field campaign. Documents the 2021 Laguna Beach multi-sensor expedition methodology, instrument suite, and initial data analysis. Co-authored with Knuth, Kugielsky, and Levy.

disclosures.title: The New Science of Unidentified Aerospace-Undersea Phenomena (UAP)
disclosures.notes: Co-author on the flagship collaborative framework paper with 30+ contributors including Kevin Knuth (lead), Hakan Kayal, Beatriz Villarroel, and Jacques Vallee. Establishes a scientific framework for empirical UAP investigation.

disclosures.title: Alternative Propulsion Engineering Conference (APEC) - UAP Instrumentation
disclosures.notes: Presented UAPx field methodology and C-TAP software approach to pixel-level UAP camera analysis.

sources.title: Initial Results UAPx Field Expedition - Progress in Aerospace Sciences (2025)

claims.claim: A multi-sensor academic field expedition to Laguna Beach, California (2021) established proof-of-concept for instrumented UAP data collection using electromagnetic, optical, and environmental detector arrays during observation windows.

C-TAP (Custom Target Analysis Protocol), combining AI classification with human verification, can perform reliable pixel-level anomaly detection on UAP camera footage by applying detector physics calibration principles.

Dark matter detector design principles - specifically sensitivity optimization and signal-to-noise characterization for rare-event physics - are directly transferable to the challenge of detecting anomalous UAP signatures in field instrumentation.

career_connections.relationship: Co-leads UAPx; institutional colleagues at University at Albany. Co-authors on both 2025 UAP papers.
Co-authors on 2025 New Science of UAP flagship paper.
First author of UAPx founding field expedition paper; operational leader of field instrumentation campaigns.
